title: Meet the Generator
concept: The generator is a neural network that takes random noise as input and learns to transform it into a realistic-looking image, starting out by producing pure garbage.

exercises.prompt: Recap: In Unit 200's computer vision work, models looked at an existing image and classified what was inside it. What's different about the network you'll meet in this unit?

options.text: It doesn't classify an existing image — it creates a brand-new image from scratch
It also classifies images, just with more categories
It only works on video, never on still images

exercises.explain: Exactly! Instead of recognizing images, this new kind of network manufactures them.

cards.title: A blank canvas and pure static
cards.body: Imagine handing an artist a canvas full of random TV static and asking them to turn it into a painting of a cat. That's the job of the network you're about to meet.

cards.title: What is the generator?
cards.body: The generator is a neural network that takes a vector of random numbers (noise) as input and outputs a full image. Its only job: transform meaningless randomness into something that looks real.

cards.title: Shaking the Etch A Sketch
cards.body: Think of the generator like a kid handed an Etch A Sketch that's already covered in random scribbled lines. At first, twisting the knobs just adds more nonsense. But with enough practice turning that same kind of random starting mess into recognizable shapes, the drawings start looking like real pictures.

cards.title: From noise to a face
cards.body: Feed the generator a list of 100 random numbers, like [0.3, -1.2, 0.8, ...]. Early in training, it might output a blurry blob of mismatched colors. After enough training, that same kind of random input produces a convincing (if fake) human face.

cards.title: It doesn't copy existing photos
cards.body: You might think the generator stitches together pieces of real training photos. It doesn't — it never even gets to look at real images directly. It only ever starts from noise and generates entirely new pixels from scratch.

cards.title: Random Noise vs. Generated Image
cards.body: What goes in vs. what should come out:

right.text:
A full image
e.g. a 64x64 pixel picture

exercises.prompt: What does the generator take as its input?

options.text: A text description of the image

exercises.explain: Right — the generator starts from randomness, not from real data.

exercises.prompt: True or false: early in training, a generator's output usually looks like a realistic image.
exercises.explain: False — early on, the generator hasn't learned anything yet, so its output is essentially garbage.

exercises.sentence: The generator's job is to transform random ___ into a realistic-looking image.
exercises.explain: The generator's entire input is noise — random numbers with no meaning on their own.

exercises.prompt: Which of these best describes a generator's very first attempt at creating an image (before any training)?

options.text: A meaningless splash of random colors and shapes
An exact duplicate of a training photo

exercises.explain: An untrained generator has learned nothing yet, so its output is essentially visual noise.

exercises.prompt: You're building a system to generate synthetic faces for a video game. You feed your brand-new, untrained generator a batch of random noise vectors. What should you expect the output to look like, and why?

options.text: Garbage — random colors and shapes with no resemblance to a face, because the generator's weights haven't learned to map noise to realistic structure yet
Photorealistic faces immediately, since neural networks come pre-trained
An exact copy of a face from the training set

exercises.explain: Exactly! An untrained generator's weights are random, so its noise-to-image mapping is meaningless — the output is garbage. Next: meet the network whose entire job is to catch the generator in the act.
